title: An Ebike Company Was Sued for Misleading Info on Safety. It Points to a Big Problem
gist: Amazon and UL settled a fraud suit against Chinese Aipas ebike makers over fake UL safety marks, but the false ads and inactive certificates persist, exposing how counterfeit safety certifications proliferate on third-party online marketplaces amid a lithium-ion battery fire crisis.

themes.theme: Enforcement vs. persistence of fake marks

insights.claim: Amazon and UL settled a July lawsuit with Chinese ebike makers behind the Aipas brand over fraudulent use of the UL safety-certification mark, obtaining a permanent injunction barring further use signed July 15, 2026.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Signals platform operators are escalating litigation against fraudulent certification, a compliance-cost tailwind for legitimate certified makers.
insights.locator: Amazon and UL, a company that tests and certifies

insights.claim: Even after agreeing to a permanent injunction, a Facebook account tied to Aipas kept running ads with UL's red logo, with one UL-mark ad active on Meta's Ad Library since April despite the January lawsuit filing.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Injunctions don't self-enforce across ad platforms; enforcement gaps mean fraudulent claims outlive legal settlements.
insights.locator: But WIRED has found a Facebook account

insights.claim: Some Aipas ebikes received SGS safety certifications only in September-November 2025, seemingly months after the company began advertising UL marks it did not hold, and three of those ebike certificates are now inactive while only the battery certificate remains active.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The timing gap shows marks were advertised before certification existed, a pattern investors can screen for when vetting hardware sellers.
insights.locator: The Amazon and UL lawsuit alleges

insights.claim: A US government report found 45 fatalities between 2017 and 2024 tied to lithium-ion battery fires in mobility devices, 19 of them ebike-related, while NYC logged 277 lithium-ion battery fires in 2024 versus 268 in 2023.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Rising fire counts create regulatory and insurance pressure that favors certified-battery supply chains.
insights.locator: A report published by the US government in April

insights.claim: Many US ebike fires are associated with aftermarket replacement battery packs rather than original equipment, per the Consumer Product Safety Commission.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Replacement-battery risk points to an underregulated aftermarket segment ripe for standards-driven consolidation.
insights.locator: Many of the country's ebike fires were associated

insights.claim: Fabricated certification marks are more prevalent on open marketplaces like Amazon because third-party sellers evade the internal compliance programs that direct retailers use to verify safety claims.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Structural trust gap favors vertically integrated direct retailers over marketplace models on safety-sensitive goods.
insights.quote: Just as consumers are more at risk to buy a counterfeit product, period, from third-party sellers, they are more at risk to buy a product with a fabricated certification mark
insights.locator: The false certification is more prevalent

insights.claim: Amazon says it invested more than a billion dollars and employed thousands of people in 2024 to fight counterfeit and fraudulent goods, scanning thousands of data points to remove them.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Quantifies the escalating fraud-policing cost embedded in marketplace margins.
insights.locator: The Aipas lawsuit says that Amazon invested

insights.claim: Product-safety fraud is described by a longtime safety expert as a common and longstanding problem that rarely surfaces publicly, implying most cases go unlitigated.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Suggests the visible lawsuits are a small fraction of systemic marketplace safety fraud.
insights.quote: this is a common problem and has been a common problem for quite some time
insights.locator: While fights over fraudulent product safety certifications

insights.claim: The CPSC in June 2026 proposed a federal mandatory safety standard for lithium-ion batteries in micromobility products, with public comments open through August, moving beyond the current patchwork of state and local rules.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A federal mandate would raise compliance barriers, pressuring low-cost uncertified importers and benefiting accredited-lab-tested brands.
insights.locator: Separately, in June, the CPSC proposed a rule

insights.claim: The CPSC in May 2026 flagged that certification-mark misuse appears to be part of broader efforts to evade US safety requirements and solicited public input on counterfeit-mark prevalence.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Regulator attention foreshadows tighter enforcement on imported safety-marked hardware.
insights.locator: The US government seems to be interested

insights.claim: New York City canceled a delivery-worker bike-swap program using Fly ebikes after Fly and UL settled for $1 million over counterfeit UL marks, with Streetsblog reporting eight lawsuits tied to fire injuries and deaths.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Municipal procurement is becoming a chokepoint that punishes fraudulent-mark suppliers.
insights.locator: Last year, New York City nixed a plan

insights.claim: UL Standards and Engagement writes standards while a now-separate firm, UL Solutions, tests products for a fee, and only companies that test through UL may use UL logos in advertising.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: The standards-vs-testing split creates confusion buyers and fraudsters exploit; understanding it is key to due diligence.

insights.claim: Companies typically choose among Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratories mostly on price rather than rigor, according to safety expert Donald Mays.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Price-driven lab selection creates a race-to-the-bottom risk in certification quality.
insights.locator: Other organizations, called Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratories

insights.claim: An inactive safety certificate does not necessarily mean a product is unsafe; certificates lapse when a company changes a design or component and declines to pay for recertification or skips required inspections.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Certificate status is a noisy but checkable signal buyers and diligence teams can verify via QR codes and lab directories.
insights.locator: An inactive safety certificate isn't necessarily a sign
